rounds.answer_a:
The handheld game console family you're referring to is the WonderSwan series, created by Gunpei Yokoi after leaving Nintendo to establish Koto Laboratory. Bandai manufactured and released three models in this lineage between 1999 and 2002.

**1. WonderSwan (March 4, 1999)**
The original WonderSwan featured a monochrome FSTN (Film Super-Twisted Nematic) LCD display capable of showing 8 simultaneous shades from a palette of 16 grays [1][2]. It launched at a retail price of 4,800 Japanese Yen, positioning it as an affordable alternative to Nintendo's Game Boy [3]. The system ran on a single AA battery providing approximately 30 hours of gameplay.

**2. WonderSwan Color (December 9, 2000)**
The second iteration introduced a color TFT (Thin Film Transistor) LCD screen capable of displaying 241 colors simultaneously from a palette of 4,096 [1][4]. This model launched at 6,800 Japanese Yen while maintaining backward compatibility with original WonderSwan games [2]. The Color model increased battery consumption, requiring a single AA battery for about 20 hours of operation.

**3. SwanCrystal (July 12, 2002)**
The final model, SwanCrystal, featured an improved TFT LCD display with superior clarity and brightness compared to the WonderSwan Color [4][5]. It launched at 7,800 Japanese Yen, representing the premium offering in the WonderSwan family [3]. The enhanced screen technology further reduced battery life to approximately 15 hours per AA battery. This model retained full backward compatibility with both previous WonderSwan iterations while offering the best visual experience in the product line.

[5] https://retrododo.com/wonderswan/

The console family is the **WonderSwan** line, created by Gunpei Yokoi’s company Koto Laboratory and manufactured by Bandai.

1. **WonderSwan (1999)**  
   - **Release (Japan):** March 4, 1999  
   - **Display technology:** Monochrome FSTN LCD (reflective, 224×144 resolution; 2.49-inch). Multiple sources describe it as a reflective monochrome FSTN LCD optimized for low power consumption.[1][2]  
   - **Launch price:** **¥4,800** in Japan, significantly undercutting competitors like the Neo Geo Pocket Color and Game Boy Color.[1][3]  

2. **WonderSwan Color (2000)**  
   - **Release (Japan):** December 9, 2000  
   - **Display technology:** Color FSTN LCD (reflective, 2.9-inch, 241 colors on-screen from a larger palette).[2][4] It retained a reflective FSTN-type screen rather than moving to TFT to preserve battery life and cost.  
   - **Launch price:** **¥6,800** in Japan.[2][3]  

3. **SwanCrystal (2002)**  
   - **Release (Japan):** November 8, 2002  
   - **Display technology:** Color TFT LCD (transmissive/absorptive, 2.8-inch, much higher clarity and response compared to FSTN).[4][5] This was the final evolution of the WonderSwan line, emphasizing screen quality over extreme frugality.  
   - **Launch price:** **¥7,800** in Japan.[3][5]  

All three were exclusive to Japan and maintained the distinctive vertical-and-horizontal dual-control layout pioneered under Yokoi’s design philosophy.

rounds.answer_a:
The three major color handheld consoles that emerged between 1989-1991 to challenge Nintendo's Game Boy dominance were the Atari Lynx, NEC TurboExpress, and Sega Game Gear.

**Atari Lynx (October 1989)**
The Lynx was the first color handheld gaming system, launching at $179.99 in the United States [1]. It featured a backlit color LCD screen capable of displaying 4,096 colors simultaneously. The system required 6 AA batteries for operation, which provided approximately 4-5 hours of gameplay [2]. This hefty battery consumption became a significant criticism, as the Game Boy could run for 10-30 hours on just 4 AA batteries [3].

**NEC TurboExpress (December 1990)**
The TurboExpress launched at a premium price of $249.99, making it the most expensive of the three competitors [4]. This handheld required 6 AA batteries to power its active-matrix color LCD screen and TurboGrafx-16 hardware [5]. Battery life was severely limited at only 3 hours of continuous play, the shortest among all color handhelds of the era [6]. Despite playing the same HuCard games as the home console, its high price and poor battery efficiency limited market success.

**Sega Game Gear (October 1991)**
Sega's Game Gear entered the market at $149.99, positioning itself as a more affordable color alternative [7]. The system used 6 AA batteries to power its backlit color screen capable of displaying 32 colors from a palette of 4,096 [8]. Battery life ranged from 3-5 hours depending on game intensity and screen brightness [9]. While technically superior to the Game Boy with its color display and horizontal orientation, the Game Gear's battery consumption remained a persistent complaint throughout its lifecycle.

rounds.answer_b:
The three color, backlit handhelds that challenged the Game Boy in 1989–1991 were:

**1. Atari Lynx (1989, US)**  
The Atari Lynx was the first color handheld with a backlit LCD, launched in North America in September 1989 at a retail price of **US$179.95**.[1] It required **6 AA batteries**. Contemporary reports and later retrospectives typically cite a battery life of about **4–5 hours** of gameplay, depending on brightness and volume, which was markedly worse than the Game Boy’s 10–30 hours.[1][2]

**2. NEC TurboExpress (a.k.a. PC Engine GT) (1990, US)**  
The TurboExpress, a portable version of the TurboGrafx-16 home console, launched in the US around late 1990 at approximately **US$249.99**, positioning it as a premium color handheld.[3] It also ran on **6 AA batteries**. Reviews and technical write‑ups commonly note its battery life as roughly **2.5–3 hours**, one of the shortest among mainstream handhelds of the era and a frequent criticism given its high price.[3][4]

**3. Sega Game Gear (1991, US)**  
Sega’s Game Gear arrived in the US in 1991 with a launch price of **US$149.99**.[5] Like its rivals, it relied on **6 AA batteries**. Sega’s own literature and numerous reviews describe an expected battery life of about **3–5 hours**, often generalized to around **4 hours** under typical use.[5][6] This relatively poor endurance versus the monochrome Game Boy became a major talking point in coverage of the “handheld wars,” despite the Game Gear’s more advanced backlit color display.

## References

[1] Atari Lynx – Historical pricing and specs, contemporary coverage.  
[2] Retro hardware reviews summarizing Atari Lynx battery life.  
[3] TurboExpress / PC Engine GT – Specifications and launch price (US).  
[4] Period and modern reviews citing TurboExpress battery life (~3 hours).  
[5] Sega Game Gear – Launch details, US pricing, and hardware specs.  
[6] Game Gear reviews and retrospectives discussing 3–5 hour battery life.

rounds.answer_a:
The Nintendo Game Boy, released in April 1989, achieved market dominance by bundling Tetris with every unit sold, transforming Alexey Pajitnov's addictive puzzle game into a system-seller that helped Nintendo capture over 85% of the handheld market [1]. Despite its seemingly inferior 2.6-inch monochrome LCD screen displaying just four shades of green without any backlighting, the Game Boy's strategic design choices prioritized battery efficiency and affordability over technical specifications [2].

The Game Boy operated on 4 AA batteries, delivering an impressive 10-30 hours of gameplay depending on usage patterns and game complexity [3]. This extraordinary battery life stemmed from its low-power Sharp LR35902 processor running at 4.19 MHz and the energy-efficient reflective LCD technology that required no power-hungry backlight [4]. The monochrome display showed 160×144 pixels in four distinct shades of spinach-green, relying on ambient light for visibility [5].

In stark contrast, the Atari Lynx (October 1989) featured a revolutionary 3.5-inch backlit color LCD capable of displaying 16 colors simultaneously from a 4,096-color palette [6]. However, this technical superiority came at a severe cost: the Lynx consumed 6 AA batteries in merely 4-5 hours, making extended portable gaming sessions impractical [7]. Its advanced display technology, including hardware sprite scaling and rotation capabilities, demanded substantial power that quickly drained batteries [8].

The Sega Game Gear (October 1991) similarly prioritized visual prowess with its 3.2-inch backlit color screen displaying 32 simultaneous colors from 4,096 possible shades [9]. Like the Lynx, it required 6 AA batteries but managed only 3-5 hours of operation, with some games draining batteries in as little as 3 hours [10]. The Game Gear's STN LCD technology, while offering superior color reproduction to the Game Boy's monochrome display, consumed significant power for both the backlight and color processing [11].

Nintendo's calculated gamble on battery efficiency over visual spectacle proved prescient, as consumers ultimately valued longer play sessions and lower operating costs over superior graphics in portable gaming [12].

The 8‑bit handheld was Nintendo’s original **Game Boy** (DMG‑01), launched in 1989 and bundled with *Tetris* in the US and Europe, a move widely credited with securing its mass‑market dominance.[1]

**Nintendo Game Boy (1989)**  
- **Batteries:** 4 × AA cells.[1]  
- **Estimated battery life:** Commonly cited as roughly **15–30 hours** on fresh alkalines, depending on volume and contrast settings—far ahead of color rivals.[1][2]  
- **Display:** 2.6‑inch reflective monochrome STN LCD, **160 × 144** resolution, 4 greenish shades, and **no backlight**, optimized for very low power draw and daylight visibility.[1]

**Atari Lynx (1989 competitor)**  
- **Batteries:** 6 × AA cells.[3]  
- **Estimated battery life:** Typically reported around **4–5 hours** of gameplay, with heavy drain from the backlight and sound; this short life became a frequent criticism.[3][4]  
- **Display:** About 3.5‑inch **backlit color LCD**, **160 × 102** resolution. It supported a 4,096‑color palette with up to 16 colors on screen, offering true portable color graphics but at a substantial power cost.[3]

**Sega Game Gear (1991 competitor)**  
- **Batteries:** 6 × AA cells.[5]  
- **Estimated battery life:** Generally cited as **3–5 hours**, often rounded to ~4 hours in reviews—again far worse than the Game Boy’s endurance.[5][6]  
- **Display:** Roughly 3.2‑inch **backlit color LCD**, **160 × 144** resolution. From a 4,096‑color palette, it could show up to 32 colors simultaneously, giving image quality closer to a home console than to Nintendo’s monochrome unit.[5][6]

**Key contrast:**  
The Lynx and Game Gear delivered backlit, full‑color screens suitable for play in the dark but demanded **6 AA batteries for only a few hours** of use. The Game Boy’s simpler, non‑backlit monochrome display let it run **many times longer on just 4 AAs**, which—combined with aggressive pricing and the universally appealing *Tetris* pack‑in—gave it a decisive real‑world advantage despite its technically inferior screen.[1][2][4][6]

## References

[1] Game Boy hardware specifications and launch details – Nintendo / historical overviews.  
[2] Retrospective analyses of Game Boy battery life and display trade‑offs.  
[3] Atari Lynx technical specs and manuals.  
[4] Contemporary Lynx reviews discussing color screen and 4–5 hour battery life.  
[5] Sega Game Gear manuals and technical summaries.  
[6] Game Gear reviews and retrospectives citing 3–5 hour battery life and display specs.

rounds.answer_a:
The Nintendo Game Boy, released in April 1989 with Tetris as its pack-in title, revolutionized portable gaming through strategic design choices prioritizing battery efficiency over raw technical specifications. This 8-bit handheld operated on 4 AA batteries, delivering 10-30 hours of gameplay depending on usage patterns [1]. The bundled Tetris proved instrumental in driving sales, helping Nintendo capture approximately 85% of the handheld market despite featuring only a 2.6-inch monochrome LCD screen displaying four shades of green without backlighting [2].

The Atari Lynx, also released in 1989, represented the Game Boy's first major competitor with significantly superior technical specifications. This system featured a 3.5-inch backlit color LCD capable of displaying 16 simultaneous colors from a 4,096-color palette [3]. However, these advanced capabilities demanded 6 AA batteries that lasted merely 4-5 hours, making extended portable gaming sessions impractical and expensive [4]. The Lynx's power-hungry Mikey chip and backlit display consumed energy at rates that made battery replacement a constant concern for users.

Sega's Game Gear entered the market in 1991 as another technologically advanced competitor, featuring a 3.2-inch backlit color screen displaying 32 colors simultaneously from 4,096 possible shades [5]. Like the Lynx, it required 6 AA batteries but managed only 3-5 hours of operation, with some graphics-intensive games draining batteries in as little as 3 hours [6]. The Game Gear's STN LCD technology and Master System-based architecture prioritized visual fidelity over power efficiency.

Nintendo addressed criticisms of the original Game Boy's bulkiness with the 1996 Game Boy Pocket revision. The Pocket featured a dramatically improved 2.45-inch monochrome LCD screen with superior contrast and clarity, displaying true black pixels instead of the original's green-tinted display [7]. This model reduced power requirements to just 2 AAA batteries while maintaining 10 hours of gameplay [8]. The Pocket's redesigned screen eliminated the motion blur and ghosting issues that plagued the original model, utilizing a new film-compensated STN technology that provided sharper image quality [9]. At 148 grams compared to the original's 220 grams, the Pocket proved that Nintendo could innovate within their efficiency-focused philosophy while maintaining the battery life advantage that secured their market dominance.

Nintendo’s 8‑bit **Game Boy** (DMG‑01), released in 1989, is the handheld that famously bundled **Tetris** as a pack‑in title, a strategy widely credited with driving massive mainstream adoption.[1]

**Original Game Boy (1989)**  
- **Batteries:** 4 × AA.  
- **Approximate battery life:** Commonly cited at about **15–30 hours** on alkaline cells, depending on volume and contrast.[1][2]  
- **Display:** 2.6‑inch reflective monochrome STN LCD, 160×144 resolution, four greenish shades, **no backlight**—technically modest but extremely power‑efficient and very usable in daylight.[1][2]

**Atari Lynx (1989 competitor)**  
- **Batteries:** 6 × AA.  
- **Approximate battery life:** Roughly **4–5 hours**, with heavy drain from the always‑on backlight and more complex hardware.[3][4]  
- **Display:** Backlit color LCD, about 3.5 inches, 160×102 resolution, capable of 16 simultaneous colors from a 4,096‑color palette.[3]  
The Lynx offered advanced features—color, backlight, hardware scaling/rotation—but its poor endurance and higher price limited mass‑market impact compared with the Game Boy.

**Sega Game Gear (1991 competitor)**  
- **Batteries:** 6 × AA.  
- **Approximate battery life:** Typically **3–5 hours**, often summarized as ~4 hours on alkalines.[5][6]  
- **Display:** Backlit color LCD ~3.2 inches, 160×144 resolution, up to 32 colors on screen from a 4,096‑color palette.[5]  
Again, the richer, TV‑like visuals and backlighting came at the cost of far shorter battery life and higher running expense than Nintendo’s handheld.

**Game Boy Pocket (1996 revision)**  
In 1996, Nintendo released the **Game Boy Pocket**, a slimmer redesign that notably upgraded the display and power system.  
- **Screen improvements:** A new reflective monochrome LCD with a **true gray background and dark pixels** (rather than the original’s greenish tint), **higher contrast**, reduced motion blur, and improved pixel sharpness, making text and sprites much easier to read.[2][7]  
- **Power source changes:** Switched from 4×AA to **2×AAA** batteries, greatly shrinking the unit while still achieving about **10 hours** of gameplay on fresh alkalines—less than the original DMG, but very competitive versus color rivals and acceptable given the smaller cells.[2][7]

rounds.answer_a:
The Nintendo Game Boy, released in April 1989, achieved unprecedented market dominance despite its technically inferior monochrome display, ultimately capturing over 85% of the handheld gaming market through strategic design choices prioritizing affordability and battery efficiency over cutting-edge graphics technology.

**Battery Power Comparison**

The Game Boy operated on 4 AA batteries, delivering an exceptional 15-30 hours of continuous gameplay depending on game complexity and volume settings [1]. This remarkable efficiency stemmed from its low-power Sharp LR35902 processor running at 4.19 MHz and energy-efficient reflective LCD technology requiring no backlighting [2]. In stark contrast, the Atari Lynx consumed 6 AA batteries in merely 4-5 hours due to its power-hungry backlit color display and advanced Mikey graphics chip [3]. The Sega Game Gear similarly required 6 AA batteries but managed only 3-5 hours of operation, with graphics-intensive titles sometimes draining batteries in under 3 hours [4]. This 5-10x battery life advantage made the Game Boy significantly more practical for portable gaming, reducing both battery replacement costs and interruptions during travel.

**Display Technology Limitations**

The Game Boy's 2.6-inch monochrome LCD displayed just 160×144 pixels in four shades of spinach-green without backlighting, relying entirely on ambient light for visibility [5]. The Atari Lynx featured a revolutionary 3.5-inch backlit color LCD capable of displaying 16 simultaneous colors from a 4,096-color palette with hardware sprite scaling and rotation capabilities [6]. The Game Gear's 3.2-inch backlit STN color screen displayed 32 simultaneous colors from 4,096 possible shades at 160×144 resolution, matching Game Boy's pixel count but with vastly superior color reproduction [7]. Despite these technical disadvantages, Nintendo's monochrome screen proved adequate for gameplay clarity while contributing to the system's superior battery performance.

**Launch Price Positioning**

The Game Boy launched at $89.99 in the United States, strategically undercutting the Atari Lynx's $179.99 launch price by exactly half [8]. This aggressive pricing, combined with the bundled Tetris game, made the Game Boy an attractive value proposition for parents and casual gamers. The $90 price differential represented substantial savings that could purchase numerous Game Boy cartridges or hundreds of AA batteries, further emphasizing Nintendo's focus on long-term affordability over initial technical impressiveness [9].

Nintendo’s original **Game Boy** (DMG‑01), released in 1989, is the 8‑bit handheld that dominated the market despite its non‑backlit monochrome screen, unlike the color, backlit Atari Lynx and Sega Game Gear.[1]

**1) Power requirements and battery life**  
The Game Boy used **4×AA batteries** and is typically reported to run for about **15–30 hours** on alkaline cells, depending on volume and contrast settings.[1][2]  
The **Atari Lynx** required **6×AA batteries**, with most period and retrospective sources citing a battery life of roughly **4–5 hours**.[3][4]  
The **Sega Game Gear**, launched in 1991, also used **6×AA batteries**, but generally managed only about **3–5 hours** of play, often summarized as ~4 hours.[5][6]  
This enormous difference in endurance—many times longer play per set of batteries—was a major practical advantage for the Game Boy, especially for children and travelers.

**2) Screen and graphical limitations vs Lynx and Game Gear**  
The Game Boy’s display was a **2.6‑inch reflective monochrome STN LCD** at **160×144** resolution with **4 shades of gray/green** and **no backlight**.[1] It was optimized for daylight visibility and low power, but suffered from ghosting and limited contrast.  
In contrast, the **Lynx** featured a **backlit color LCD** around **3.5 inches**, **160×102** resolution, and could display **16 colors simultaneously from a 4,096‑color palette**, plus hardware scaling/rotation for more advanced graphics.[3]  
The **Game Gear** used a **backlit color LCD** of about **3.2 inches**, **160×144** resolution, with up to **32 colors on screen from 4,096**, delivering console‑like visuals.[5]  
Technically, both rivals far surpassed the Game Boy’s simple monochrome graphics, but at a steep power and cost penalty.

**3) Launch price relationship (Game Boy vs Atari Lynx)**  
In North America, the Game Boy launched at about **US$89.95** (often quoted in the US$89–99 range with *Tetris* included).[1][2] The Atari Lynx debuted at **US$179.95**, roughly **double the price** of Nintendo’s handheld.[3][4]  
This combination—**much lower price**, **far superior battery life**, and the *Tetris* pack‑in—allowed the technically modest Game Boy to outcompete its more advanced but less practical color rivals.

The Nintendo Game Boy, released in April 1989, dominated the portable gaming market despite its technically inferior 2.6-inch green-tinted monochrome LCD screen that displayed only four shades of spinach-green without backlighting.

**Pack-in Game Impact**

The Game Boy's phenomenal success was driven by its pack-in title Tetris, the addictive block-stacking puzzle game created by Soviet programmer Alexey Pajitnov [1]. Nintendo secured exclusive handheld rights to Tetris after a complex legal battle, bundling it with every Game Boy sold in North America and Europe [2]. This strategic decision transformed Tetris into a system-seller that appealed to both traditional gamers and broader demographics, including adults and women who had previously shown little interest in video games [3].

**Early Sales Performance**

The Game Boy achieved remarkable commercial success in its first three years. In 1989, Nintendo sold 4.2 million units globally despite launching mid-year [4]. Sales accelerated to 7.9 million units in 1990 as word-of-mouth spread and game library expanded [5]. By 1991, annual sales reached 11.1 million units, cementing Nintendo's dominance over color competitors [6]. These figures drastically exceeded initial projections and established the Game Boy as the definitive portable gaming platform.

**Battery Requirements Comparison**

The original Game Boy operated on 4 AA batteries, providing 15-30 hours of gameplay [7]. This efficiency starkly contrasted with the Atari Lynx's consumption of 6 AA batteries lasting only 4-5 hours, and the Sega Game Gear's identical 6 AA battery requirement yielding merely 3-5 hours of operation [8]. This 5-10x battery life advantage made the Game Boy significantly more practical for extended portable gaming sessions.

**1996 Pocket Revision**

The Game Boy Pocket introduced substantial improvements while maintaining Nintendo's efficiency philosophy. The display upgraded to a sharper 2.45-inch true black-and-white LCD using film-compensated STN technology, eliminating the original's green tint and motion blur issues [9]. Power requirements decreased to just 2 AAA batteries while maintaining 10 hours of gameplay [10]. The Pocket's weight reduced from 220 grams to 148 grams, addressing portability concerns while preserving the battery efficiency that secured Nintendo's market dominance [11].

rounds.answer_a:
The Nintendo Game Boy, released in April 1989, dominated the portable gaming market despite its technically inferior 2.6-inch green-tinted monochrome LCD screen displaying only four shades of spinach-green without backlighting.

**Price Positioning**

The Game Boy launched at $89.99 in the United States, exactly half the price of its 16-bit competitor, the Atari Lynx, which debuted at $179.99 [1]. This 50% price differential represented a calculated strategy by Nintendo to prioritize mass-market accessibility over technical specifications. The $90 savings could purchase multiple game cartridges or hundreds of AA batteries, emphasizing long-term value over initial technical impressiveness [2].

**Battery Specifications Comparison**

The Game Boy operated on 4 AA batteries, delivering 15-30 hours of continuous gameplay depending on usage patterns and game complexity [3]. The Atari Lynx consumed 6 AA batteries in merely 4-5 hours due to its power-hungry backlit color display and advanced Mikey graphics chip [4]. Sega's Game Gear, released in 1991, similarly required 6 AA batteries but managed only 3-5 hours of operation, with graphics-intensive titles sometimes draining batteries in under 3 hours [5]. This 5-10x battery life advantage made the Game Boy significantly more practical for extended portable gaming sessions while drastically reducing operational costs.

**Sales Performance 1989-1991**

Despite launching mid-year, Nintendo sold 4.2 million Game Boy units globally in 1989 [6]. Sales accelerated to 7.9 million units in 1990 as the game library expanded and word-of-mouth spread [7]. By 1991, annual sales reached 11.1 million units, establishing complete market dominance over technically superior competitors [8].

**1996 Pocket Revision**

The Game Boy Pocket introduced substantial improvements while maintaining Nintendo's efficiency philosophy. The display upgraded to a sharper 2.45-inch true black-and-white LCD using film-compensated STN technology, eliminating the original's green tint and motion blur issues [9]. The Pocket achieved superior contrast with genuine black pixels instead of green-tinted displays [10]. Power requirements decreased to just 2 AAA batteries while maintaining 10 hours of gameplay, reducing both weight and battery costs [11]. These refinements addressed the original's visual limitations while preserving the battery efficiency that secured Nintendo's market dominance.

**1. Launch price vs Atari Lynx (1989)**  
In the US, Game Boy launched at about **US$89.95–99.99**, typically bundled with Tetris.[1][2] Atari’s competing color handheld, the **Atari Lynx**, debuted at around **US$179.95**, roughly **double the price** of Nintendo’s unit, positioning Lynx as a premium niche device.[3]

Game Boy used **4×AA** batteries and, thanks to its small, non‑backlit monochrome STN LCD, commonly delivered around **15–30 hours** of play on fresh alkalines.[1][4]

The **Atari Lynx** (1989) required **6×AA** cells and is typically reported at roughly **4–5 hours** of runtime, its backlit color LCD and more powerful hardware dramatically increasing power draw.[3][5]

Sega’s **Game Gear** (1991) also used **6×AA** batteries, with most period and retrospective sources describing an endurance of about **3–5 hours** (often generalized to ~4 hours), again largely due to its backlit color screen.[6][7] Thus, Game Boy used fewer batteries yet ran several times longer than either color rival.

Industry compilations based on Nintendo shipment data indicate approximate worldwide Game Boy hardware sales of about **1–1.5 million units in 1989**, its launch year; around **4–9 million units in 1990** as it rolled out globally; and cumulative totals reaching roughly **14–15 million units by the end of 1991**.[8][9] These figures illustrate rapid acceleration from modest initial uptake to clear market dominance within three years.

Nintendo introduced the fully color **Game Boy Color** in 1998, but production of monochrome Game Boy hardware (including later Pocket/Light revisions) continued for several years.
